title: Helping a Parent Adjust to Memory Care in the First Weeks
summary: The adjustment period to memory care is often harder than families expect, but it typically does ease — here's what helps.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Helping a Parent Adjust to Memory Care in the First Weeks | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: The adjustment period to memory care is often harder than families expect, but it typically does ease — here's what helps.
whatYouNeedToKnow: An initial period of increased confusion or distress is common and often temporary.
Visiting too frequently in the very first days can sometimes make adjustment harder, not easier.
Consistency and familiar routines help more than frequent, disruptive visits.

paragraphs: It's common, and understandably distressing, for a parent to seem more confused or upset in the first days or weeks after a memory care move — this is often a temporary reaction to the environmental change rather than a sign the placement is wrong.

Somewhat counterintuitively, some communities recommend a brief adjustment period with less frequent visiting initially, since new visits can sometimes retrigger the desire to "go home" just as things are starting to settle. Ask the staff for their specific guidance on this.

nextSteps: Ask the community's specific advice on visit frequency during the adjustment period.
Bring familiar objects to help create a sense of continuity.
Give the adjustment real time before assuming the placement isn't working.
disclaimer: General guidance — every adjustment period is different, and ongoing distress beyond several weeks is worth discussing directly with the care team.
